title: Inspector General for Ukraine Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new government office to watch over the money America sends to Ukraine. The office will check how all the aid money is being used and make sure it is spent properly. Every three months, this office will write reports about what they find and share them with Congress and the public. The office will work for five years and will cost seventy million dollars to run.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Inspector General for Ukraine Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Inspector General for Ukraine
sections.text: This section creates a new government office called the Office of the Inspector General for Ukraine. The President will choose someone to lead this office, called the Inspector General for Ukraine. The Senate must approve this choice. The person chosen must have good character and proven skills in areas like accounting, auditing, money analysis, law, management, public administration, or investigations. The President must pick someone within thirty days after this law is passed. This person will be paid the same as other high-level government officials at Executive Schedule level four. They cannot participate in political activities while doing this job. The Inspector General will hire two assistant inspectors general. One will supervise audits of programs and operations that use money given to support Ukraine's military and non-military needs. The other will supervise investigations of these same programs and operations. The Inspector General will report to both the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Defense. However, no one from the Defense Department, State Department, or US Agency for International Development can stop the Inspector General from starting, doing, or finishing any audit or investigation related to Ukraine support money, or from issuing subpoenas during these audits or investigations. The Inspector General's main job is to conduct, supervise, and coordinate audits and investigations of how money given for Ukraine's military and non-military support is treated, handled, and spent. This includes overseeing and accounting for how the money is used, monitoring and reviewing contracts paid for with this money, watching how money and information move between US government agencies and private groups, keeping records to help with future audits and investigations, looking into overpayments like duplicate payments or billing and any unethical or illegal actions by federal employees or contractors, monitoring all military and non-military activities paid for with this money, and tracking all deadly and non-deadly security assistance given by the US including checking if all end-use requirements are followed. The Inspector General must set up and maintain systems, procedures, and controls to do these duties properly. The Inspector General also has the same duties and responsibilities as other inspectors general under federal law. When doing this work, the Inspector General must coordinate with and get cooperation from the inspectors general of the Defense Department, State Department, and US Agency for International Development. The Inspector General has the same authorities as other inspectors general under federal law, including the power to issue subpoenas. The Inspector General must follow federal audit standards when doing audits. The Inspector General can hire officers and employees needed to do the job, following federal hiring rules and pay scales. The Inspector General can also use special hiring authorities for temporary appointments, but these cannot last longer than when this office ends. The Inspector General can hire experts and consultants at daily rates up to the equivalent of a GS-15 federal employee. The Inspector General can make contracts for audits, studies, analyses, and other services with public agencies and private people if Congress provides the money in advance. The Secretary of State or Secretary of Defense must give the Inspector General proper office space at State or Defense Department locations in Ukraine or at a US military base in Europe, along with equipment, supplies, and communication services. They must also provide proper support for audits, investigations, and related activities in Ukraine. Any federal agency must give information or help to the Inspector General when asked, unless existing law prevents it. If any agency unreasonably refuses to help, the Inspector General must report this to the Secretary of State or Secretary of Defense and to the appropriate congressional committees right away. Every three months, the Inspector General must give a report to the appropriate congressional committees. The report must summarize what the Inspector General did during that quarter and what happened with programs and operations funded with Ukraine support money. Each report must include detailed information about all obligations, expenditures, and revenues related to Ukraine support, including how appropriated funds were used, operating expenses of agencies getting Ukraine support money, details about major contracts and grants including their amount and scope and how the government found contractors and why they did not use full open competition if they did not, a comparison of military and non-military support the US gave to Ukraine versus what other NATO countries gave including any US money used to backfill allied contributions, and an evaluation of whether Ukraine's government is meeting all requirements for getting US money including any areas of concern. The Inspector General must publish each report on a public website in English and other languages widely used in Ukraine. Reports must be unclassified but can include classified sections if necessary. Nothing in this allows disclosure of information specifically prohibited by law, required to be protected by Executive order for national defense or security or foreign affairs, or part of an ongoing criminal investigation. The Inspector General must also give each report to the Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense. Within thirty days of getting a report, these Secretaries must send any comments on the report to congressional committees. These comments must be unclassified but can include classified sections if needed. Any Member of Congress can ask to see these comments including classified parts. Within sixty days after giving a report to congressional committees, the Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense must make copies of the report available to the public upon request at reasonable cost. They must do the same with their comments on reports within sixty days of giving comments to congressional committees. The President can waive the requirement to make reports or comments available to the public if the President decides this is justified for national security reasons. The President must publish notice of each waiver in the Federal Register no later than when the report or comments are given to congressional committees. The report and comments must specify if waivers were used and for which parts. The President cannot waive the requirement for the Secretaries to submit comments to Congress. In this section, amounts appropriated or otherwise made available for Ukraine support means money appropriated or made available on or after January first two thousand twenty two for specific programs like the Ukraine Security Assistance Initiative, foreign military financing accessed by Ukraine's government, Presidential drawdown authority, defense institution building programs, building partner capacity programs, International Military Education and Training programs, and US European Command, plus money for military, economic, reconstruction, or humanitarian support of Ukraine under any account not already listed. Appropriate congressional committees means the Appropriations, Armed Services, Foreign Relations, and Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committees in the Senate and the Appropriations, Armed Services, Foreign Affairs, and Oversight and Government Reform Committees in the House of Representatives. Congress authorizes seventy million dollars for fiscal year two thousand twenty five to carry out this section. To pay for this, the amount authorized for the Economic Support Fund for Ukraine under a previous law is reduced by seventy million dollars. The Office of the Inspector General for Ukraine will end five years after this law is passed. Before the office ends, the Inspector General must prepare and give congressional committees a final forensic audit report on programs and operations funded with Ukraine support money.
